**Pashto**[[b]](#cite_note-7) ([/ˈpʌʃtoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[6]](#cite_note-8)[[4]](#cite_note-ahd-5)[[5]](#cite_note-OEDuk-6)[/ˈpæʃtoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*PASH-toh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[c]](#cite_note-10) پښتو, *Pəx̌tó*, [[pəʂˈto, pʊxˈto, pəʃˈto, pəçˈto]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Pashto "Help:IPA/Pashto")) is an [Eastern Iranian language](/wiki/Eastern_Iranian_languages "Eastern Iranian languages") in the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"), natively spoken in northwestern [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") and southern and eastern [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"). It has official status in Afghanistan. It was also known in historical [Persian literature](/wiki/Persian_literature "Persian literature") as **Afghani** (افغانی, *Afghāni*).[[8]](#cite_note-Leyden-11)

Spoken as a native language mainly by ethnic [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns"), it is one of the two official [languages of Afghanistan](/wiki/Languages_of_Afghanistan "Languages of Afghanistan") alongside [Dari](/wiki/Dari "Dari"),[[9]](#cite_note-AO-12)[[10]](#cite_note-AC-13)[[11]](#cite_note-14) and the second-largest [language in Pakistan](/wiki/Languages_of_Pakistan "Languages of Pakistan"), spoken mainly in [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") and the northern districts of [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan").[[1]](#cite_note-census-1) Likewise, it is the primary language of the [Pashtun diaspora](/wiki/Pashtun_diaspora "Pashtun diaspora") around the world. The total number of Pashto speakers is estimated at around 35 million to 55 million.[[12]](#cite_note-ELL2-15)[[13]](#cite_note-16)[[14]](#cite_note-17) Pashto is "one of the primary markers of ethnic identity" amongst Pashtuns.[[15]](#cite_note-18)

## Geographic distribution

Further information: [Languages of Afghanistan](/wiki/Languages_of_Afghanistan "Languages of Afghanistan") and [Languages of Pakistan](/wiki/Languages_of_Pakistan "Languages of Pakistan")

A national language of Afghanistan,[[16]](#cite_note-Pashto-language-19) Pashto is primarily spoken in the east, south, and southwest, but also in some northern and western parts of the country. The exact number of speakers is unavailable, but different estimates show that Pashto is the [mother tongue](/wiki/First_language "First language") of 35–45%[[17]](#cite_note-20)[[18]](#cite_note-UCLA-21)[[19]](#cite_note-Iranica-languages-22) of the total [population of Afghanistan](/wiki/Demography_of_Afghanistan "Demography of Afghanistan").

In Pakistan, Pashto is spoken by 18.1% of its population,[[1]](#cite_note-census-1) mainly in the northwestern province of [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") and northern districts of [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan") province. Pashto speakers are also found in the [Punjab province](/wiki/Punjab_(Pakistan) "Punjab (Pakistan)"), [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad") and in the other major cities of Pakistan, most notably [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi"), Sindh,[[20]](#cite_note-pbs-23)[[21]](#cite_note-The_National-24)[[22]](#cite_note-tribune.com.pk-25)[[23]](#cite_note-thefridaytimes.com-26) which may have the largest Pashtun population in the world.[[24]](#cite_note-27)

Other communities of Pashto speakers are found in [India](/wiki/India "India"),[[25]](#cite_note-28) [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan"),[[26]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-2000-29) and northeastern [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") (primarily in [South Khorasan Province](/wiki/South_Khorasan_Province "South Khorasan Province") to the east of [Qaen](/wiki/Qaen "Qaen"), near the Afghan border).[[27]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-Iran-30) In India most ethnic Pashtun (Pathan) peoples speak the geographically native [Hindi-Urdu language](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") rather than Pashto, but there are small numbers of Pashto speakers, such as the [Sheen Khalai](/wiki/Sheen_Khalai "Sheen Khalai") in [Rajasthan](/wiki/Rajasthan "Rajasthan"),[[28]](#cite_note-31) and the Pathan community in the city of [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata"), often nicknamed the *Kabuliwala* ("people of [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul")").[[29]](#cite_note-32)[[30]](#cite_note-33) Pashtun diaspora communities in other countries around the world speak Pashto, especially the sizable communities in the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates")[[31]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-UAE-34) and [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia").

### Afghanistan

Pashto is one of the two official languages of Afghanistan, along with [Dari Persian](/wiki/Dari_Persian "Dari Persian").[[32]](#cite_note-socioling-35) Since the early 18th century, [the monarchs of Afghanistan](/wiki/List_of_heads_of_state_of_Afghanistan "List of heads of state of Afghanistan") have been ethnic Pashtuns (except for [Habibullāh Kalakāni](/wiki/Habibull%C4%81h_Kalak%C4%81ni "Habibullāh Kalakāni") in 1929).[[33]](#cite_note-rahman-36) Persian, the literary language of the royal court,[[34]](#cite_note-37) was more widely used in government institutions, while the [Pashtun tribes](/wiki/Pashtun_tribes "Pashtun tribes") spoke Pashto as their [native tongue](/wiki/First_language "First language"). King [Amanullah Khan](/wiki/Amanullah_Khan "Amanullah Khan") began promoting Pashto during his reign (1926–1929) as a marker of ethnic identity and as a symbol of "official nationalism" after the [Third Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Afghan_War "Third Anglo-Afghan War") in 1919, which restored Afghan control over their foreign policy.[[33]](#cite_note-rahman-36) In the 1930s, a movement began to take hold to promote Pashto as a language of government, administration, and art with the establishment of a Pashto Society *Pashto Anjuman* in 1931[[35]](#cite_note-38) and the inauguration of the [Kabul University](/wiki/Kabul_University "Kabul University") in 1932 as well as the formation of the [Pashto Academy](/wiki/Pashto_Academy "Pashto Academy") (Pashto *Tolana)* in 1937.[[36]](#cite_note-hussain-39) Muhammad Na'im Khan, the minister of education between 1938 and 1946, inaugurated the formal policy of promoting Pashto as Afghanistan's national language, leading to the commission and publication of Pashto textbooks.[[37]](#cite_note-40) The Pashto Tolana was later incorporated into the Academy of Sciences Afghanistan in line with Soviet model following the [Saur Revolution](/wiki/Saur_Revolution "Saur Revolution") in 1978.[[38]](#cite_note-41)

Although officially supporting the use of Pashto, the Afghan elite regarded Persian as a "sophisticated language and a symbol of cultured upbringing".[[33]](#cite_note-rahman-36) King [Zahir Shah](/wiki/Mohammed_Zahir_Shah "Mohammed Zahir Shah") (reigning 1933–1973) thus followed suit after his father [Nadir Khan](/wiki/Mohammed_Nadir_Shah "Mohammed Nadir Shah") had decreed in 1933 that officials were to study and utilize both Persian and Pashto.[[39]](#cite_note-42) In a 1936 [royal decree](/wiki/Royal_decree "Royal decree"), Zahir Shah formally granted Pashto the status of an official language,[[40]](#cite_note-43) with full rights to use in all aspects of government and education, even though the ethnically Pashtun royal family and bureaucrats mostly spoke Persian.[[36]](#cite_note-hussain-39) Thus Pashto became a [national language](/wiki/National_language "National language") and a symbol for [Pashtun nationalism](/wiki/Pashtun_nationalism "Pashtun nationalism").

The [constitutional assembly](/wiki/1964_Constitution_of_Afghanistan "1964 Constitution of Afghanistan") reaffirmed the status of Pashto as an official language in 1964 when Afghan Persian was officially renamed to [Dari](/wiki/Dari_language "Dari language").[[41]](#cite_note-44)[[42]](#cite_note-45) Since the return of the Taliban regime in 2021, although they have reaffirmed both Dari and Pashto as the official languages, critics accuse the Taliban of marginalizing Persian in favor of Pashto.[[43]](#cite_note-46)[[44]](#cite_note-47) The lyrics of the [national anthem of Afghanistan](/wiki/This_Is_the_Home_of_the_Brave "This Is the Home of the Brave") under the Taliban are also in Pashto.

### Pakistan

In [British India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), the 1920s saw the blossoming of Pashto language in the then [NWFP](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province "North-West Frontier Province"): [Bacha Khan](/wiki/Bacha_Khan "Bacha Khan") in 1921 established the *Anjuman-e Islah al-Afaghina* (Society for the Reformation of Afghans) to promote Pashto as an extension of Pashtun culture; around 80,000 people attended the Society's annual meeting in 1927.[[45]](#cite_note-48) In 1955, Pashtun intellectuals including [Abdul Qadir](/wiki/Abdul_Qadir_(academician) "Abdul Qadir (academician)") formed the [Pashto Academy Peshawar](/wiki/Pashto_Academy "Pashto Academy") on the model of Pashto Tolana formed in Afghanistan.[[46]](#cite_note-49) In 1974, the Department of Pashto was established in the University of Balochistan for the promotion of Pashto.[[47]](#cite_note-50)

In Pakistan, Pashto is the first language of around 18.1% of its population (per the 2023 census).[[1]](#cite_note-census-1) However, [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") and [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") are the two official languages of Pakistan. Pashto has no official status at the federal level. Pashto is the regional language of [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") and north [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan").[[48]](#cite_note-51) Yet, the primary medium of education in government schools in Pakistan is Urdu.[[49]](#cite_note-52)[[50]](#cite_note-53) The lack of importance given to Pashto and its neglect has caused growing resentment amongst Pashtuns.[[51]](#cite_note-Sociolinguistic_Survey_of_Northern_Pakistan-54)[[52]](#cite_note-55)[[53]](#cite_note-56)[[54]](#cite_note-57) It is noted that Pashto is taught poorly in schools in Pakistan.[[55]](#cite_note-58) Moreover, in government schools material is not provided for in the Pashto dialect of that locality, Pashto being a dialectically rich language.[[56]](#cite_note-59) Further, researchers have observed that Pashtun students are unable to fully comprehend educational material in Urdu.[[57]](#cite_note-60)

Professor [Tariq Rahman](/wiki/Tariq_Rahman "Tariq Rahman") states:[[58]](#cite_note-61)

> "The government of Pakistan, faced with irredentist claims from Afghanistan on its territory, also discouraged the Pashto Movement and eventually allowed its use in peripheral domains only after the Pakhtun elite had been co-opted by the ruling elite...Thus, even though there is still an active desire
> among some Pakhtun activists to use Pashto in the domains of power, it is more of a symbol of Pakhtun identity than one of nationalism."

— Tariq Rahman, The Pashto language and identity-formation in Pakistan

Robert Nicols states:[[59]](#cite_note-62)

> "In the end, national language policy, especially in the field of education in the NWFP, had constructed a type of three tiered language hierarchy. Pashto lagged far behind Urdu and English in prestige or development in almost every domain of political or economic power..."

— Language Policy and Language Conflict in Afghanistan and Its Neighbors, Pashto Language Policy and Practice in the North West Frontier Province

Although Pashto is used as a medium of instruction in schools for Pashtun students, which results in better understanding and comprehension for students when compared to using Urdu, the government of Pakistan has introduced Pashto only at the primary levels in state-run schools.[[60]](#cite_note-63) Taimur Khan remarks: "the dominant Urdu language squeezes and denies any space for Pashto language in the official and formal capacity. In this contact zone, Pashto language exists but in a subordinate and unofficial capacity".[[61]](#cite_note-64)

## History

Some linguists have argued that Pashto is descended from [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan") or a variety very similar to it, while others have attempted to place it closer to [Bactrian](/wiki/Bactrian_language "Bactrian language").[[62]](#cite_note-Darmesteter_1890-65)[[63]](#cite_note-66)[[64]](#cite_note-67) However, neither position is universally agreed upon. What scholars do agree on is the fact that Pashto is an [Eastern Iranian language](/wiki/Eastern_Iranian_languages "Eastern Iranian languages") sharing characteristics with Eastern Middle Iranian languages such as Bactrian, [Khwarezmian](/wiki/Khwarezmian_language "Khwarezmian language") and [Sogdian](/wiki/Sogdian_language "Sogdian language").[[65]](#cite_note-68)[[66]](#cite_note-Iranica-Pashto-69)

[Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo"), who lived between 64 BC and 24 CE, explains that the tribes inhabiting the lands west of the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") were part of [Ariana](/wiki/Ariana "Ariana"). This was around the time when the area inhabited by the Pashtuns was governed by the [Greco-Bactrian Kingdom](/wiki/Greco-Bactrian_Kingdom "Greco-Bactrian Kingdom"). From the 3rd century CE onward, they are mostly referred to by the name *Afghan* (*Abgan*).[[73]](#cite_note-Habibi-76)[[74]](#cite_note-Britannica-Abgan-77)[[75]](#cite_note-Abgan-78)[[8]](#cite_note-Leyden-11)

[Abdul Hai Habibi](/wiki/Abdul_Hai_Habibi "Abdul Hai Habibi") believed that the earliest modern Pashto work dates back to [Amir Kror Suri](/wiki/Amir_Kror_Suri "Amir Kror Suri") of the early [Ghurid](/wiki/Ghurid "Ghurid") period in the 8th century, and they used the writings found in [Pə́ṭa Xazāná](/wiki/Pata_Khazana "Pata Khazana"). Pə́ṭa Xazāná (پټه خزانه) is a Pashto [manuscript](/wiki/Manuscript "Manuscript")[[76]](#cite_note-79) claimed to be written by Mohammad Hotak under the patronage of the Pashtun emperor [Hussain Hotak](/wiki/Hussain_Hotak "Hussain Hotak") in [Kandahar](/wiki/Kandahar "Kandahar"); containing an anthology of Pashto poets. However, its authenticity is disputed by scholars such as [David Neil MacKenzie](/wiki/David_Neil_MacKenzie "David Neil MacKenzie") and Lucia Serena Loi.[[77]](#cite_note-80)[[78]](#cite_note-Lucia_Serena_Loi_1987,_p._33-81) [Nile Green](/wiki/Nile_Green "Nile Green") comments in this regard:[[79]](#cite_note-82)

> "In 1944, Habibi claimed to have discovered an eighteenth-century manuscript anthology containing much older biographies and verses of Pashto poets that stretched back as far as the eighth century. It was an extraordinary claim, implying as it did that the history of Pashto literature reached back further in time than Persian, thus supplanting the hold of Persian over the medieval Afghan past. Although it was later convincingly discredited through formal linguistic analysis, Habibi's publication of the text under the title Pata Khazana ('Hidden Treasure') would (in Afghanistan at least) establish his reputation as a promoter of the wealth and
> antiquity of Afghanistan's Pashto culture."

— Afghan History Through Afghan Eyes

From the 16th century, Pashto poetry became popular among the Pashtuns. Some notable poets and authors who wrote in Pashto include [Pir Roshan](/wiki/Pir_Roshan "Pir Roshan") (inventor of the [Pashto alphabet](/wiki/Pashto_alphabet "Pashto alphabet")), [Khushal Khan Khattak](/wiki/Khushal_Khan_Khattak "Khushal Khan Khattak"), [Rahman Baba](/wiki/Rahman_Baba "Rahman Baba"), [Nazo Tokhi](/wiki/Nazo_Tokhi "Nazo Tokhi"), and [Ahmad Shah Durrani](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_Durrani "Ahmad Shah Durrani"), founder of the [Durrani Empire](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire"), precursor of the modern state of Afghanistan. The Pashtun literary tradition grew in the backdrop to weakening Pashtun power following Mughal rule: Khushal Khan Khattak used Pashto poetry to rally for Pashtun unity and Pir Bayazid as an expedient means to spread his message to the Pashtun masses.[[80]](#cite_note-83)

For instance Khushal Khattak laments in :[[81]](#cite_note-84)

> "The Afghans (Pashtuns) are far superior to the Mughals at the sword,
>
> Were but the Afghans, in intellect, a little discreet.
> If the different tribes would but support each other,
>
> Kings would have to bow down in prostration before them"

— Khushal Khan Khattak, Selections from the Poetry of the Afghans

## Grammar

Main article: [Pashto grammar](/wiki/Pashto_grammar "Pashto grammar")

Pashto is a [subject–object–verb](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93object%E2%80%93verb "Subject–object–verb") (SOV) language with [split ergativity](/wiki/Split_ergativity "Split ergativity"). In Pashto, this means that the verb agrees with the subject in transitive and intransitive sentences in non-past, non-completed clauses, but when a completed action is reported in any of the past tenses, the verb agrees with the subject if it is intransitive, but with the object if it is transitive.[[16]](#cite_note-Pashto-language-19) [Verbs](/wiki/Verb "Verb") are inflected for present, simple past, past progressive, present perfect, and past perfect tenses. There is also an inflection for the [subjunctive mood](/wiki/Subjunctive_mood "Subjunctive mood").

Nouns and adjectives are [inflected](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection") for two [genders](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") (masculine and feminine),[[82]](#cite_note-85) two [numbers](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number") (singular and plural), and four [cases](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case") (direct, oblique, ablative, and vocative). The possessor precedes the possessed in the genitive construction, and [adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") come before the [nouns](/wiki/Noun "Noun") they modify.

Unlike most other Indo-Iranian languages, Pashto uses all three types of [adpositions](/wiki/Adpositional_phrase "Adpositional phrase")—prepositions, postpositions, and circumpositions.

## Vocabulary

See also: [Pashto\_dialects § Lexemes](/wiki/Pashto_dialects#Lexemes "Pashto dialects")

In Pashto, most of the native elements of the lexicon are related to other [Eastern Iranian languages](/wiki/Eastern_Iranian_languages "Eastern Iranian languages").[[66]](#cite_note-Iranica-Pashto-69) As noted by Josef Elfenbein, "Loanwords have been traced in Pashto as far back as the third century B.C., and include words from Greek and probably Old Persian".[[86]](#cite_note-89) For instance, [Georg Morgenstierne](/wiki/Georg_Morgenstierne "Georg Morgenstierne") notes the Pashto word مېچن *mečə́n* i.e. *a hand-mill* as being derived from the Ancient Greek word μηχανή (*mēkhanḗ*, i.e. a device).[[87]](#cite_note-90) Post-7th century borrowings came primarily from [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and [Hindi-Urdu](/wiki/Hindi-Urdu "Hindi-Urdu"), with Arabic words being borrowed through Persian,[[88]](#cite_note-91) but sometimes directly.[[89]](#cite_note-92)[[90]](#cite_note-93) Modern speech borrows words from English, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), and [German](/wiki/German_language "German language").[[91]](#cite_note-Penzl2-94)

However, a remarkably large number of words are unique to Pashto.[[92]](#cite_note-BensonKosonen20132-95)[[93]](#cite_note-Ehsan_M_Entezar_2008_89-96)

Here is an exemplary list of Pure Pashto and borrowings:[[94]](#cite_note-A_dictionary_of_the_Puk'hto,_Pus'hto,_or_language_of_the_Afghans-97)[[95]](#cite_note-auto-98)

Due to the incursion of [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and [Persianized-Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") in modern speech, [linguistic purism](/wiki/Linguistic_purism "Linguistic purism") of Pashto is advocated to prevent its own vocabulary from dying out.[[93]](#cite_note-Ehsan_M_Entezar_2008_89-96)[*[self-published source](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[96]](#cite_note-BensonKosonen2013-99)[[97]](#cite_note-100)

### Classical vocabulary

There is a lot of old vocabulary that has been replaced by borrowings e.g. پلاز *plâz*[[98]](#cite_note-101) 'throne' with تخت *takht*, from Persian.[[99]](#cite_note-102)[[100]](#cite_note-103) Or the word يګانګي *yagānagí*[[101]](#cite_note-104) meaning 'uniqueness' used by Pir Roshan Bayazid.[[102]](#cite_note-:0-105) Such classical vocabulary is being reintroduced to modern Pashto.[[103]](#cite_note-106) Some words also survive in dialects like ناوې پلاز 'the bride-room'.[[104]](#cite_note-107)

Example from [Khayr al-Bayān](/wiki/Khayr_al-Bay%C4%81n "Khayr al-Bayān"):[[102]](#cite_note-:0-105)

:   ... بې يګانګئ بې قرارئ وي او په بدخوئ کښې وي په ګناهان
:   **Transliteration:** ... **be-yagānagə́i**, be-kararə́i wi aw pə badxwə́i kx̌e wi pə gunāhā́n
:   **Translation:** "... without **singularity/uniqueness**, without calmness and by bad-attitude are on sin ."

## Writing system

Main article: [Pashto alphabet](/wiki/Pashto_alphabet "Pashto alphabet")

Pashto employs the [Pashto alphabet](/wiki/Pashto_alphabet "Pashto alphabet"), a modified form of the [Perso-Arabic alphabet](/wiki/Perso-Arabic_alphabet "Perso-Arabic alphabet") or [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script").[[105]](#cite_note-108) In the 16th century, [Bayazid Pir Roshan](/wiki/Pir_Roshan "Pir Roshan") introduced 13 new letters to the Pashto alphabet. The alphabet was further modified over the years.

The Pashto alphabet consists of 45 to 46 letters[[106]](#cite_note-109) and 4 diacritic marks. Latin Pashto is also used.[[107]](#cite_note-110)[[108]](#cite_note-111)[[109]](#cite_note-112) In Latin transliteration, stress is represented by the following markers over vowels: **ә́**, **á**, **ā́**, **ú**, **ó**, **í** and **é**. Main article: [Pashto dialects](/wiki/Pashto_dialects "Pashto dialects")

## Literary Pashto

Literary Pashto is the artificial variety of Pashto that is used at times as [literary register](/wiki/Literary_language "Literary language") of Pashto. It is said to be based on the North Western dialect, spoken in the central [Ghilji](/wiki/Ghilji "Ghilji") region. Literary Pashto's vocabulary, also derives from other dialects.[[111]](#cite_note-Coyle_2014-114)

### Criticism

There is no actual Pashto that can be identified as "Standard" Pashto, as Colye remarks:[[111]](#cite_note-Coyle_2014-114)

> "Standard Pashto is actually fairly complex with multiple varieties or forms. Native speakers or researchers often refer to Standard Pashto without specifying which variety of Standard Pashto they mean...people sometimes refer to Standard Pashto when they mean the most respected or favorite Pashto variety among a majority of Pashtun speakers."

— Placing Wardak among Pashto Varieties, page 4

According to David MacKenzie, there is no real need to develop a "Standard" Pashto:[[112]](#cite_note-115)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

> "The morphological differences between the most extreme north-eastern and south-western dialects are comparatively few and unimportant. The criteria of dialect differentiation in Pashto are primarily phonological. With the use of an alphabet which disguises these phonological differences the language has, therefore, been a literary vehicle, widely understood, for at least four centuries. This literary language has long been referred to in the West as 'common' or 'standard' Pashto without, seemingly, any real attempt to define it."

— A Standard Pashto, page 231

## Literature

Main article: [Pashto literature and poetry](/wiki/Pashto_literature_and_poetry "Pashto literature and poetry")

Pashto speakers have long had a tradition of [oral literature](/wiki/Oral_literature "Oral literature"), including [proverbs](/wiki/Afghan_proverbs "Afghan proverbs"), stories, and poems. Written Pashto literature saw a rise in development in the 17th century, mostly due to poets like [Khushal Khan Khattak](/wiki/Khushal_Khan_Khattak "Khushal Khan Khattak") (1613–1689), who, along with [Rahman Baba](/wiki/Rahman_Baba "Rahman Baba") (1650–1715), is widely regarded as among the greatest Pashto poets. From the time of [Ahmad Shah Durrani](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_Durrani "Ahmad Shah Durrani") (1722–1772), Pashto has been the language of the court. The first Pashto teaching text was written during the period of Ahmad Shah Durrani by Pir Mohammad Kakar with the title of *Maʿrifat al-Afghānī* ("The Knowledge of Afghani [Pashto]"). After that, the first grammar book of Pashto [verbs](/wiki/Verb "Verb") was written in 1805 under the title of *Riyāż al-Maḥabbah* ("Training in Affection") through the patronage of Nawab Mahabat Khan, son of [Hafiz Rahmat Khan](/wiki/Hafiz_Rahmat_Khan "Hafiz Rahmat Khan"), chief of the [Barech](/wiki/Barech "Barech"). Nawabullah Yar Khan, another son of Hafiz Rahmat Khan, in 1808 wrote a book of Pashto words entitled *ʿAjāyib al-Lughāt* ("Wonders of Languages").

### Poetry example

An excerpt from the *Kalām* of [Rahman Baba](/wiki/Rahman_Baba "Rahman Baba"):

زۀ رحمٰن پۀ خپله ګرم يم چې مين يم  
چې دا نور ټوپن مې بولي ګرم په څۀ

**Pronunciation**: [zə raˈmɑn pə ˈxpəl.a ɡram jəm t͡ʃe maˈjan jəm  
t͡ʃe dɑ nor ʈoˈpən me boˈli ɡram pə t͡sə]

**Transliteration:** *Zə Rahmā́n pə xpə́la gram yəm če mayán yəm  
Če dā nor ṭopə́n me bolí gram pə tsə*

**Translation:** "I Rahman, myself am guilty that I am a lover,  
On what does this other universe call me guilty."

### Proverbs

See also: [Pashto literature and poetry § Proverbs](/wiki/Pashto_literature_and_poetry#Proverbs "Pashto literature and poetry")

Pashto also has a rich heritage of proverbs (Pashto *matalúna*, sg. *matál*).[[113]](#cite_note-116)[[114]](#cite_note-117) An example of a proverb:

اوبه په ډانګ نه بېلېږي

**Transliteration:** O*bә́ pə ḍāng nə beléẓ̌i*

**Translation:** "One cannot divide water by [hitting it with] a pole."
